---
title: "Ch 1 — Lines and Planes in Space"
subtitle: "Parallel, intersecting, skew — and the three positions a line can take with a plane"
lesson-id: "kg2.lines-and-planes"
tier: "kg2"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book II (Stereometry)"
kiselev-chapter: 1
kiselev-chapter-title: "Lines and planes"
kiselev-sections: "Stereometry §§373–415 (positions of lines and planes; perpendicular line to a plane; dihedral and trihedral angles)"
prerequisites: [kg1.parallels, kg1.perpendiculars]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Are the *parallel-line* theorems automatic for you in 2D? See
   [`kg1.parallels`](../../tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/04-parallels/index.qmd).
2. Can you state the *perpendicular-bisector locus theorem* from
   [`kg1.perpendiculars`](../../tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/03-perpendiculars/index.qmd)?
3. Can you visualise three points in space that are *not* on a
   single line — and the unique plane they determine?
4. Are you comfortable with the everyday objects: a wall (plane),
   the floor (plane), the line where they meet (their
   intersection)?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

Two lines in space. In the plane, two distinct lines either *meet
at a point* or are *parallel*. In space, there is a **third
possibility**: they neither meet nor are parallel. Such lines are
called **skew**.

```{ojs}
viewof config = Inputs.radio(
  ["intersecting (meet at a point)", "parallel (same direction, no meeting)", "skew (no meeting, no parallel)"],
  {value: "intersecting (meet at a point)", label: "Configuration:"}
)
```

```{ojs}
{
  // simple "isometric" sketch of a cube and two highlighted lines
  const w = 460, h = 320, padX = 40, padY = 40;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 7]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 5]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  // cube edges (8 vertices, isometric projection)
  const project = (x, y, z) => [x + 0.5 * y, z + 0.4 * y];
  const V = [
    [0,0,0],[3,0,0],[3,3,0],[0,3,0],
    [0,0,3],[3,0,3],[3,3,3],[0,3,3]
  ];
  const P = V.map(([x,y,z]) => project(x, y, z));
  const edges = [
    [0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,0],
    [4,5],[5,6],[6,7],[7,4],
    [0,4],[1,5],[2,6],[3,7]
  ];
  for (const [a,b] of edges) {
    svg.append("line")
      .attr("x1", xS(P[a][0])).attr("y1", yS(P[a][1]))
      .attr("x2", xS(P[b][0])).attr("y2", yS(P[b][1]))
      .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-width", 1);
  }

  // highlight two lines depending on config
  const drawHi = (a, b, c) => {
    svg.append("line")
      .attr("x1", xS(P[a][0])).attr("y1", yS(P[a][1]))
      .attr("x2", xS(P[b][0])).attr("y2", yS(P[b][1]))
      .attr("stroke", c).attr("stroke-width", 3);
  };

  if (config.startsWith("intersecting")) {
    // edges that share a vertex: 0-1 and 0-3 (both at vertex 0)
    drawHi(0, 1, "#26a");
    drawHi(0, 3, "#a26");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
      .attr("font-size", 12).text("Two edges meeting at one vertex — intersecting lines.");
  } else if (config.startsWith("parallel")) {
    // 0-1 and 3-2 are parallel (opposite edges of the bottom face)
    drawHi(0, 1, "#26a");
    drawHi(3, 2, "#a26");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
      .attr("font-size", 12).text("Two opposite edges of one face — parallel lines.");
  } else {
    // 0-1 (bottom front) and 6-7 (top back) — skew
    drawHi(0, 1, "#26a");
    drawHi(6, 7, "#a26");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
      .attr("font-size", 12).text("Bottom-front edge and top-back edge — skew lines (different directions, no meeting).");
  }

  return svg.node();
}
```

The new word is **skew**. Two skew lines have *no meeting point*
and yet are *not parallel* — they slip past each other in space.

## Wonder

1. In the plane, two distinct lines must meet or be parallel. In
   space, that is no longer true. **Why** does the third option
   (skew) exist?
2. A point and a line *not* through the point — how many planes
   contain *both*?
3. A line is *parallel to a plane* if it never meets the plane.
   What is the *intersection* of a non-parallel line with a plane?
4. A line is *perpendicular to a plane* if it makes a right angle
   with **every** line in the plane through the foot. Is checking
   *one* line enough? *(Trick question.)*

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Two lines determine a *plane* only if they meet or are
parallel. Skew lines force a 3D-only configuration. (2) **Exactly
one** plane contains a given line and a point not on it.
(3) Either a single point or the whole line (if the line lies in
the plane). (4) Checking one line is **not enough** — but checking
*two distinct* lines through the foot **is**. The proof is one of
the prettiest in Stereometry.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Three points determine a plane

Three points $A, B, C$ in space, *not* collinear, determine **exactly
one** plane.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
This is **Stereometry's first axiom** (Planimetry §3 generalised):
*through any three non-collinear points there passes exactly one
plane.* Existence and uniqueness are taken as foundational. *(A
plane is essentially a 2D Euclidean copy embedded in 3D.)*
*(Stereometry §374.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Three configurations of two lines

Show that any two distinct lines in space fall into exactly one of
three classes: (a) **intersecting** (meet at a point), (b) **parallel**
(same direction, no meeting, lie in a common plane), (c) **skew**
(no meeting, no common plane).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
*Two lines lie in a common plane* iff they are intersecting or
parallel. The contrapositive is *skew = no common plane = no
meeting*.
</details>
<parameter>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Coplanar case.** If the two lines are in a common plane, they are
either parallel or intersecting (the planar dichotomy from
Planimetry).

**Non-coplanar case.** If they share no plane, they cannot meet —
since a single intersection point + the rest of either line +
non-trivial extension forces both to lie in the plane through that
point and any other two points (one from each line), contradiction.
And they cannot be parallel — parallel lines lie in a common plane
by definition.

So two distinct lines are exactly one of: intersecting, parallel, or
skew. **QED.** *(Stereometry §378.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 — Three positions of a line and a plane

Classify the possible relationships between a line $\ell$ and a
plane $\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Three cases:
- $\ell$ **lies in** $\pi$ (every point of $\ell$ is in $\pi$).
- $\ell$ **intersects** $\pi$ at exactly one point.
- $\ell$ **is parallel to** $\pi$ (no intersection — $\ell$ and
  $\pi$ have no common point).

There are no other cases: a line either intersects the plane in
the empty set (parallel), one point, or has at least two points in
the plane (in which case the whole line is in the plane, since two
points determine a line within a plane).
*(Stereometry §380.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 — Line perpendicular to a plane

Define: a line $\ell$ is **perpendicular to a plane** $\pi$ at a
point $F \in \ell \cap \pi$ if $\ell$ is perpendicular to **every**
line in $\pi$ through $F$. Show that this is equivalent to: $\ell$
is perpendicular to **two** distinct lines in $\pi$ through $F$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
*If $\ell$ is perpendicular to two distinct lines $m_1, m_2$ in
$\pi$ through $F$, then any third line $m$ in $\pi$ through $F$ can
be expressed as a "linear combination" of $m_1$ and $m_2$ — and the
perpendicularity to $\ell$ is preserved by this combination.*
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The harder direction: *two perpendiculars suffice*. Let $\ell$ be
perpendicular at $F$ to two distinct lines $m_1, m_2 \subset \pi$
through $F$. Take any third line $m$ in $\pi$ through $F$.

Choose points $A_1 \in m_1, A_2 \in m_2$ at unit distance from $F$.
Any point $X$ on $m$ at unit distance from $F$ has the form
$X = a A_1 + b A_2$ with $a^2 + b^2 = 1$ (parameterising the
unit circle in $\pi$ in the basis $A_1, A_2$).

Take any point $P$ on $\ell$, $P \ne F$. Use the *three-point
perpendicular formula* (a coordinate / vector argument): $|PX|^2 =
|PF|^2 + |FX|^2$ iff $\angle PFX = 90°$. The hypothesis
$\angle PFA_1 = \angle PFA_2 = 90°$ gives $|PA_i|^2 = |PF|^2 +
|FA_i|^2$ for $i = 1, 2$. By a careful expansion (treating $X$ as a
linear combination), one finds $|PX|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FX|^2$,
i.e. $\ell \perp m$ at $F$. Since $m$ was arbitrary, $\ell$ is
perpendicular to *every* line through $F$ in $\pi$ — and so $\ell
\perp \pi$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §389 — the **perpendicular-line-
to-plane theorem**.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 — Existence and uniqueness of perpendicular line through a point

Through a given point $P$, there exists exactly one line
perpendicular to a given plane $\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
*If $P \in \pi$:* erect two perpendiculars in $\pi$ to two
intersecting reference lines through $P$ in $\pi$, then use Problem
4 in reverse.

*If $P \notin \pi$:* drop a perpendicular from $P$ to $\pi$ —
existence by reflection symmetry; uniqueness by an angle-sum
argument analogous to Planimetry §57.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Existence (point on the plane).* Let $P \in \pi$. Choose two
distinct lines $m_1, m_2 \subset \pi$ through $P$. Erect a line
$\ell_1$ in the plane through $P$ and $m_1$ (the "vertical plane"
above $m_1$) perpendicular to $m_1$. Similarly $\ell_2$ in the
plane through $P$ and $m_2$ perpendicular to $m_2$. Now
$\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ are two distinct lines through $P$ in the
"vertical pencil" above $P$. By a standard argument (the perpendicular
plane through $P$), there is a unique line $\ell$ through $P$
perpendicular to both $m_1$ and $m_2$ in $\pi$ — and by Problem 4
this $\ell$ is perpendicular to $\pi$.

*Uniqueness.* Suppose two distinct perpendiculars $\ell_1, \ell_2$
existed through $P$. Each is perpendicular to every line in $\pi$
through $P$. The plane through $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ would intersect
$\pi$ in some line $m$ through $P$, and both $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$
would be perpendicular to $m$ in this *plane through $\ell_1, \ell_2$*
— so $\ell_1 = \ell_2$ (uniqueness of the planar perpendicular).
*(Stereometry §391.)*

The case $P \notin \pi$ is similar; one drops a perpendicular foot.
**QED.**
</details>

### Problem 6 — The perpendicular is the shortest

The perpendicular from a point $P$ to a plane $\pi$ has the
**shortest length** among all segments from $P$ to points in $\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is exactly the planar shortest-segment theorem (Ch 3 of
Planimetry) lifted to 3D — the same argument works on the right
triangle formed by $P$, the perpendicular foot $F$, and any other
point $Q \in \pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $F$ be the foot of the perpendicular from $P$ to $\pi$, and
$Q \in \pi$ any other point. The triangle $\triangle PFQ$ has
$\angle PFQ = 90°$ (since $\ell \perp \pi$ implies $\ell$ is
perpendicular to *every* line in $\pi$ through $F$, including
line $FQ$). By the planar Pythagoras: $|PQ|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FQ|^2 >
|PF|^2$. So $|PQ| > |PF|$. **The perpendicular minimises.**
*(Stereometry §392.)*
</details>

### Problem 7 — Dihedral angles

Two half-planes sharing a common edge $\ell$ form a **dihedral
angle**. Its measure is the planar angle one sees by intersecting
both half-planes with a plane perpendicular to $\ell$. Show that
this measure does not depend on the choice of the perpendicular
plane.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two perpendicular cross-sections of the dihedral angle are
parallel to each other (both perpendicular to $\ell$). The two
planar angles are corresponding under that parallelism.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Take two perpendicular planes $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$, both
perpendicular to $\ell$ at points $F_1, F_2$ respectively. The two
half-planes' intersections with $\sigma_1$ are two rays out of
$F_1$ at some angle $\theta_1$; similarly $\theta_2$ at $F_2$. By
construction, $\sigma_1 \parallel \sigma_2$ (both perpendicular to
$\ell$). Each pair of rays in $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are
*translations* of each other along $\ell$ — so $\theta_1 =
\theta_2$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §400 — well-defined dihedral
measure.)*
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Eratosthenes of Cyrene** (~240 BCE), librarian at Alexandria,
estimated the Earth's circumference using only the geometry of two
[lines](/glossary.qmd#line) in space. He noted that on the summer
solstice the Sun was directly overhead at Syene (modern Aswan) — a
vertical stick cast no shadow — while at Alexandria, ~800 km north,
a stick cast a shadow corresponding to about $7.2°$ — one-fiftieth of
a full turn. So the full circumference was about $50 \times 800 =
40\,000$ km. The modern value is $40\,075$ km. The whole calculation
hinges on the parallel-lines-in-space and skew-line geometry of this
chapter.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The plane has *two* relationships between two lines: meet, or
parallel. Space adds a *third* — **skew**. And the
[right-angle](/glossary.qmd#right-angle) condition between a line
and a plane needs only two perpendiculars in the plane to be
certified — Kiselev's *two-perpendicular theorem* — the synthetic
ancestor of the linear-algebra fact that *orthogonality to a
spanning set is orthogonality to the whole subspace*.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definitions (Stereometry §§373–415).**

- **Plane:** a flat 2D surface in 3D space; uniquely determined by
  three non-collinear points.
- **Two lines in space:** intersecting, parallel, or **skew**
  (none of the above).
- **Line and plane:** the line lies in the plane, intersects it at
  one point, or is parallel to the plane.
- **Two planes:** intersect in a line, or are parallel.
- **Line perpendicular to plane** ($\ell \perp \pi$): $\ell$ is
  perpendicular to every line in $\pi$ through the foot.
  *Equivalently,* perpendicular to **two** distinct lines in $\pi$
  through the foot (the *two-perpendiculars theorem*).
- **Dihedral angle:** the figure formed by two half-planes sharing a
  common edge. Its measure is the planar angle of any cross-section
  perpendicular to the edge.

**Theorems.**

1. *Three non-collinear points uniquely determine a plane.*
2. *Two lines in space are intersecting, parallel, or skew —
   no other option.*
3. *A line perpendicular to two distinct lines in a plane (through
   their foot) is perpendicular to every line in the plane through
   the foot.*
4. *Through any point in space, there is a unique line
   perpendicular to a given plane.*
5. *The perpendicular from a point to a plane is the shortest
   segment from the point to the plane.*
6. *The dihedral-angle measure is well-defined: independent of the
   perpendicular cross-section chosen.*

:::

A verse:

> *In the plane, lines meet or run parallel — that's all.*
> *In space, a third path appears — skew, two strangers passing.*
> *Perpendicular to two lines through one foot? Perpendicular to all.*
> *And the perpendicular is shortest, like its planar elder, never lapsing.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A line $\ell$ pierces a plane $\pi$ at a point $F$. Two lines
> $m_1, m_2 \subset \pi$ both pass through $F$ and are
> perpendicular to $\ell$. Is $\ell$ necessarily perpendicular to
> $\pi$?

If $m_1$ and $m_2$ are *distinct*, then yes — by the two-perpendicular
theorem (Problem 4). If they are the same line, no — $\ell$ is
perpendicular only to that one line in $\pi$, and to many other
choices of one-line-in-$\pi$ it might not be perpendicular.

**Conclusion: yes, provided $m_1 \ne m_2$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Two lines in space pass through the same point and are not
parallel. Are they skew?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No.** They meet at that point, so they are *intersecting*, not
skew. Skew lines never meet.
</details>

**W2.** Through a given point in space and a given plane, how many
perpendiculars to the plane through that point exist?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Exactly one** (Problem 5). The point can be on the plane or off
it; either way, one perpendicular.
</details>

**W3.** Two parallel planes are cut by a third plane. The
intersections are two lines. What is their relationship?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**They are parallel.** *(Each lies in the cutting plane and is
parallel to the same direction inherited from the parallel
planes.)*
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that if a line is perpendicular to a plane, then any
plane *containing* that line is perpendicular to the original
plane.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two planes are perpendicular iff their dihedral angle is $90°$.
Construct the perpendicular-cross-section.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\ell \perp \pi$ at $F$, and let $\sigma$ be any plane
containing $\ell$. The dihedral angle between $\sigma$ and $\pi$
is measured by intersecting both with a plane perpendicular to
their common edge (which is $\sigma \cap \pi$, a line through
$F$). The cross-section through $F$ perpendicular to this edge
contains $\ell$ (since $\ell \perp \pi \perp$ edge means
$\ell$ is perpendicular to the edge — wait, let me check: $\ell$
perpendicular to $\pi$ means $\ell$ is perpendicular to the edge,
which lies in $\pi$. So $\ell$ is perpendicular to the edge of the
dihedral angle, and the cross-section perpendicular to that edge
through $F$ contains $\ell$). The cross-section also intersects
$\pi$ in a line perpendicular to the edge (in $\pi$). The angle
between $\ell$ and this line in $\pi$ is — by the perpendicularity
$\ell \perp \pi$ — exactly $90°$. So the dihedral angle is $90°$,
i.e. $\sigma \perp \pi$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §405.)*
</details>

**P2.** Show that two planes both perpendicular to a third plane
need *not* be parallel.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at two walls of a room — they are both perpendicular to the
floor, but they are perpendicular to **each other**, not parallel.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Consider three mutually perpendicular planes (walls and floor of a
room). Two of the walls are both perpendicular to the floor, but
they are perpendicular to *each other*, not parallel. Hence
**two planes perpendicular to a third can be perpendicular,
parallel, or even neither (intersecting at any angle other than 0°
or 90°).**
</details>

**P3.** Show that the **distance from a point to a plane** is the
length of the perpendicular from the point to the plane (and is
well-defined — independent of any other configuration).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Direct application of Problem 6 (the perpendicular minimises) and
Problem 5 (the perpendicular is unique). The minimum is achieved
once, at the perpendicular foot.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *(The three-perpendiculars theorem.)* A point $P$ off a
plane $\pi$ has perpendicular foot $F$ in $\pi$. From $F$, drop a
perpendicular within $\pi$ to a line $\ell \subset \pi$, foot $G$.
Show that $PG \perp \ell$ — i.e. the line from $P$ to the foot of
the perpendicular within $\pi$ is also perpendicular to $\ell$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$PF \perp \pi$ means $PF \perp \ell$. $FG \perp \ell$ by
construction. So $\ell$ is perpendicular to two lines $PF, FG$
through $G$ in the plane $PFG$ — and thus perpendicular to *every*
line in that plane through $G$, in particular $PG$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$PF \perp \pi$ ⇒ $PF \perp \ell$ (since $\ell \subset \pi$).
$FG \perp \ell$ by construction. Hence $\ell$ is perpendicular to
both $PF$ and $FG$, two lines in plane $PFG$ meeting at $G$. By
Problem 4 (two-perpendiculars), $\ell \perp$ plane $PFG$, hence
$\ell \perp PG$ (any line through $G$ in plane $PFG$).
**QED.** *(Stereometry §394 — the **three-perpendiculars theorem**,
the geometry-engine of every planar-shadow problem.)*
</details>

**S2.** A regular tetrahedron $ABCD$ has all four faces equilateral
triangles of side $s$. Find the *distance from any vertex to the
opposite face*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Drop the perpendicular from $A$ to the centroid of $\triangle BCD$.
Use the equilateral-triangle centroid (at distance $s/\sqrt 3$ from
each vertex) and Pythagoras.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The centroid of equilateral $\triangle BCD$ is at distance
$\dfrac{s}{\sqrt 3}$ from each of $B, C, D$. The apex $A$ has
$|AB| = s$. Pythagoras: $h^2 + \left(\dfrac{s}{\sqrt 3}\right)^2 =
s^2$, so $h^2 = s^2 - \dfrac{s^2}{3} = \dfrac{2 s^2}{3}$, hence
$h = s \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{s \sqrt 6}{3}$. *(A canonical
3D-Pythagoras computation; Kiselev §403.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Stereometry*, §§385–410.

**K1.** Through a point $P$ off a plane $\pi$, all the segments to
points on a *circle* in $\pi$ have the same length. Show that the
perpendicular from $P$ to $\pi$ lands on the **centre** of that
circle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The set of points in $\pi$ equidistant from $P$ is the locus of
the foot $F$'s perpendicular cone — which intersects $\pi$ in a
circle centred at $F$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $F$ be the foot of the perpendicular from $P$ to $\pi$. For
any point $Q \in \pi$, $|PQ|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FQ|^2$. So $|PQ|$ is a
*monotonic* function of $|FQ|$. The set $\{Q : |PQ| = $ constant$\}$
is therefore $\{Q : |FQ| = $ constant'$\}$ — a *circle* in $\pi$
centred at $F$. **QED.** *(Stereometry §394.)*
</details>

**K2.** Two skew lines $\ell_1, \ell_2$ in space have a unique
**common perpendicular** — a single line meeting both at right
angles. Sketch this and explain why it must exist and be unique.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Existence.* Through $\ell_1$ pass a plane $\pi_1$ parallel to
$\ell_2$ (this exists: take any plane containing $\ell_1$ and a
line through any point of $\ell_1$ parallel to $\ell_2$).
$\ell_2$ projects onto a line $\ell_2'$ in $\pi_1$ parallel to
$\ell_2$. The lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2'$ in $\pi_1$ either meet
at a point $X$ or are parallel; if parallel, $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$
are parallel (contradiction). So they meet at $X$. The
perpendicular to $\pi_1$ at $X$ — call it $m$ — is perpendicular to
$\ell_1$ (since $\ell_1 \subset \pi_1$) and perpendicular to
$\ell_2'$, hence (by translation argument) to $\ell_2$.

*Uniqueness.* Two common perpendiculars would force $\ell_1$ and
$\ell_2$ to lie in the plane of those two perpendiculars,
contradicting "skew".
*(Stereometry §397 — the **common-perpendicular theorem**.)*
</details>

**K3.** A point $P$ in space is *equidistant* from three given
points $A, B, C$. Show that $P$ lies on the *perpendicular line*
through the **circumcentre** of $\triangle ABC$ (perpendicular to
the plane $ABC$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|PA| = |PB|$ and $|PA| = |PC|$ — interpret these as planar
perpendicular-bisector loci, in a *3D-perpendicular-plane* sense.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|PA| = |PB|$: the locus is the *perpendicular plane* through the
midpoint of $AB$ — i.e. the plane perpendicular to $AB$ through its
midpoint $M_{AB}$. Similarly $|PA| = |PC|$ gives the perpendicular
plane through $M_{AC}$. Both planes contain the perpendicular line
through the circumcentre of $\triangle ABC$ to the plane $ABC$.
The intersection of the two planes is exactly that perpendicular
line. **QED.** *(Stereometry §410 — the **3D circumcentre
theorem**, basis for the *circumsphere* of a tetrahedron.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book II — Stereometry*, §§373–415 (Sumizdat 2008). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/%5BKiselev,Givental%5DKiselev's_Geom2.pdf).
S1 is the *three-perpendiculars theorem*, §394 — Kiselev's
favourite theorem of Stereometry's first chapter, used heavily
later.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. Two distinct [lines](/glossary.qmd#line) in space are *intersecting*,
   *parallel*, or **skew** — three options, not two as in the plane.
2. A line is perpendicular to a plane iff it makes a
   [right angle](/glossary.qmd#right-angle) with **two** distinct
   lines in the plane through the foot — *not just one*.
3. The perpendicular from a [point](/glossary.qmd#point) to a plane
   is the *shortest* segment from the point to the plane — the same
   minimisation principle as in 2D, lifted one dimension up.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Two non-parallel lines must meet."** True in the plane;
   *false* in space, where skew lines exist.
2. **"A line perpendicular to one line in a plane is perpendicular
   to the plane."** **False.** Need *two distinct* lines in the
   plane through the foot — the two-perpendicular theorem (Problem
   4) is about *two*, not one.
3. **"Perpendicular planes both contain the same perpendicular
   line."** No — *perpendicular planes* are two planes whose
   dihedral angle is $90°$; they do not share a common perpendicular
   line in general.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state and prove the *two-perpendicular theorem*
   (Problem 4) — the heart of perpendicularity in space — without
   notes?
2. Did the *three-perpendiculars theorem* (S1) feel like one move,
   or did you need scaffolding?
3. Can you sketch a configuration of two skew lines and identify
   their common perpendicular?

- **3 yes:** move on to *Polyhedra* (Ch 2).
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 4 — it is the chapter's core machinery.
- **0–1 yes:** the four core problems (3, 4, 5, 7) form one logical
  block; replay them.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg1.parallels`](../../tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/04-parallels/index.qmd) —
  parallel-line theorems lifted to 3D (parallel planes,
  parallel lines in different planes).
- [`kg1.perpendiculars`](../../tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/03-perpendiculars/index.qmd) —
  perpendicular-as-shortest-segment, lifted to 3D.

### What this unlocks

- **`kg2.polyhedra`** *(Ch 2, planned)* — prisms, pyramids, regular
  polyhedra all rely on perpendicular-line-to-plane (for heights)
  and dihedral angles.
- [`kg2.cylinder-cone`](../03-cylinder-cone/index.qmd) — the axis
  of a cylinder is perpendicular to its base; understanding
  perpendicular lines in space is the prerequisite.
- [`t4.vectors-2d-3d`](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd) —
  the synthetic geometry here translates directly to vector dot
  products and cross products.
- [`cbse12.lines-in-3d`](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/11a-lines-in-3d/index.qmd) —
  CBSE Class 12's coordinate version of the same theorems.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book II — Chapter 1: Lines and planes (§§373–415).**
  Givental translation, Sumizdat 2008. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/%5BKiselev,Givental%5DKiselev's_Geom2.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`cbse11.three-d-geometry-intro`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/11-three-d-geometry-intro/index.qmd)
  (CBSE 11 introduction);
  [`cbse12.lines-in-3d`](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/11a-lines-in-3d/index.qmd)
  and
  [`cbse12.planes-in-3d`](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/11b-planes-in-3d/index.qmd)
  (board-exam coordinate treatments).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development
  of the *three-perpendiculars theorem* (§394).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every 3D engine relies on **plane equations**,
**ray-plane intersections**, and **perpendicular foot
calculations**. Lighting (the *normal vector* is the perpendicular
to a surface), collision (perpendicular distance from a point to a
wall), and physics (resolution of forces in 3D) all use the
synthetic theory you just built.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The **two-perpendiculars theorem** (Problem 4) is the
synthetic seed of **linear algebra** in $\mathbb R^3$: a vector is
orthogonal to a 2D subspace iff it is orthogonal to two
spanning vectors. The same statement, from synthetic to vector to
inner-product space, is one continuous arc of mathematical
abstraction.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the §§373–415 ordering and the proof of
  Problem 4); Hilbert's *Grundlagen* (which laid out 3D axioms
  rigorously); the three-perpendiculars theorem from §394 — Kiselev's
  favourite move.
- **Problem 4's proof** I gave is sketched; the full proof Kiselev
  presents uses an explicit *isosceles-triangle in the perpendicular
  plane* argument that takes about half a page. Worth reading in
  full.
- **What this lesson does not do:** treat the parallel postulate's
  3D version (the existence of parallel planes through a point off
  a plane) as a separate axiom — it follows from the planar version
  applied to the right cross-section.
:::
